tokens: We're hustling right now, and we're going to eat breakfast, have a little talk with you, and get about our business.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: What's the local time down there, Paul?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: For information, Pete, can you see the ALSEP out the LM window?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: When you get a chance, what I would like for you to do is: they are not getting the readings they expected from the CCIG (Cold Cathode Ion Gauge).
If you can, we would like you to give a look-see at it with the monocular to see whether you can tell whether it is right side up and whether the port has been opened or not.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We left that in a rather precarious position due to that cable last night, and it wouldn't surprise me if it is not upside down, face down, because that is exactly where it wanted to go.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: They've given a command to open the ports, and the pressure has not gone down in it at the rate they expected.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: When we left it last night, Houston, we left it sort of on its back facing about 80 degrees from the horizontal.
That was the best we could do.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Unfortunately, Houston, it is on the opposite side of the SIDE, which is supposed to be lined up with my view of it.
It does look like it's roughly the same position as we left it last night.
We'll run over there after we get out and look.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I have got a late change for you that came in a couple of hours ago.
On the rocks you are bringing back in the
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I didn't quite get the end of that.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Houston, while you are working that problem, I would like to know if there are any restrictions on when we go over the sill.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Stand by, Pete, and we will give you the word on that.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I would like to go as soon as I can get ready without hurrying.
And I've kind of got the suspicion, looking over the Prep card, (that) a good bit of this stuff is done, and it is pretty much a deal of hooking up the PLSSs and going.
And I think we are going to be able to get out somewhere around 131:30 to 132, and let me look at the timeline and see what we should be doing then normally.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Whenever you're ready, at your own pace, you can go over the sill.
Of course, we do want to talk to you about the briefing on the traverse before you go out.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I'll give you a call in about 3 or 4 minutes.
We have some sprucing up to do; and, while we are eating breakfast, you can give us the hot word on geology.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Also give us some word on the families, if you would.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Did you get that on the allowable weight of the materials to be brought back in the (spare)
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: How far down on the scale is that?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: They want the TV camera brought back, so I've got some changes to your surface checklist to cover stowing it for return when you are ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Down under the block that contains words "rest periods," about the fourth item down is "voice-to-voice." We want to insert in there a step to "stow utility towels from the hammocks in the upper boot box".
These will be used, as I will describe to you later, to protect the TV camera when you stow it for return.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We already took them out to use to keep ourselves nice and clean.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: On the right-hand column under EVA-2 Prep, about the eighth or ninth line down, it says "remove
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: On the right-hand column under "LMP", next to the last step after "Verify circuit breaker," insert a step to open the TV circuit breaker.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: In other words, you say to turn the TV on to begin with and then before I get out to open it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I should have made that clear.
They want to try one more look to see if something short of a miracle occurred, and then you will open the circuit breaker again before you get out.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Be sure to remind me because it's not on my cuff checklist, and I won't have this one (the LM checklist) out.
And what's the latest thinking on that, too?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: You mean on why it didn't work, Al?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Man, I worried about that one all night.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Now, we're going to have you use your Surveyor dismantling tool, I guess, on this TV camera - the (bolt) cutters - to get it apart.
So add a couple of steps here, Pete, at the end of your list.
We want you to cut the TV cable on the spacecraft side of the camera connector, below the adapter.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: And then stow the TV camera in the ETB.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We'll remind you of these steps since they're not on your checklist.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Also mention...(Stops to listen to Weitz) That's right, because they're not on the cuff.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: In the right-hand column, the fourth or fifth line up, after "Unstow 70 millimeter cameras," add a step to "unstow the TV camera from the ETB and stow on the engine cover."
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Let me read it over here a minute, and I'll paraphrase it for you.
We want to fold the TV camera.
We're going to stow it in the lithium hydroxide canister (means "bracket").
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Do you want us to pack it with the towels as best we can, huh?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And they want it...(Pause) Just wrap the remaining...Fold the handle; wrap the cable around the camera as best you can; and stow it in the lithium-hydroxide-canister bracket on the engine cover and stow it with the lens up.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Now has anybody checked to make sure that we can fit the camera and all the cables and stuff in that fitting?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: You probably can, but I don't know.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: The only reason I wondered is in case it...We'll make it work.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: It's been checked out; the lens will protrude up out of the canister stowage about 6 (or) 7 inches and they then want you to wrap some utility straps around the end of the camera to hold it in place.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: And one other thing, on your tiedown of this bag with the extra rocks in it, they want you to run an additional strap...Stand by one.
After you get the bag secured on the deck there between you, they want an additional strap run from the straps holding the bag down up to the ISA D-ring for additional support.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: That takes care of our procedure changes this morning.
I'll give your families a call and get the word back up to you in about 5 or 10 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: What's the Yankee Clipper doing, sleeping?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: He's cutting off a few Z's there.
(Pause) Clipper's scheduled to sleep until 131:30, and our intention now is to let him sleep until then, if he does.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I got a couple of messages for you here I'll read up to you.
"The officers and crew of the USS Independent or - correction - of the USS Intrepid extend their best wishes to all three of you.
They're following your progress closely as you write another intrepid chapter in American history.
And, from the blue Caribbean, they wish you smooth sailing on your voyage across the vast ocean of space and their thoughts and prayers are with you."
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I'm going to bring the computer out of P06 and put it back to bed again.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I've got another one of those 1105 alarms, which is "uplink too fast".
I don't know why that happens all the time, but it does.
I'm going to go ahead and put it back to sleep again.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I have a consumables update for you, if you want it, Pete; and, after that, I've got some lift-off block data.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Go ahead with the consumables update, and then the block data.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: RCS (Reaction Control System) Alpha is 80 percent; Bravo is 76; oxygen is 76 (percent in the descent tank) and 96 (percent in the ascent tank); water, 47 and 99; and your amp hours (remaining) is 850 and 572.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: The lift-off block data for Rev 25, which is T13, is 132:11:35; Rev 26/T14, is 134:09:59; Rev 27/T15, 136:08:25; Rev 28/T16, is 138:06:50.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We copied the consumables updates, and here are the lift-off times 132:11:35.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I have a couple of words on your rendezvous radar self-test and your
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Your rendezvous radar self-test was good.
The checklist had the spec ("specification") values, not the actual values, which is the reason for the difference.
They ran through them on the ground and it checked out four oh (4.0, meaning "perfect").
Your tapemeter perilune checks were also good.
MPAD ran a solution through on the ground here using the actual CSM state vectors.
They had been slightly perturbed, and they came up with the same values off the chart as you did.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: (Coughs) How about giving me the word on geology now?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: He (Ed Gibson)'ll be with you in a minute, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We're ready to go with the traverse plan.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We can pick it up at LSE 7-6G.
I think that is the easiest way to follow it on your map.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I have it right in my hand.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: First of all, the two prime sites we consider on here are Bench and Sharp craters.
We could pretty much follow the traverse which we discussed before.
What I'd like to do is give you the additional information that you don't have on your sheet and also, perhaps, to discuss how we'll fit the ALSEP revisit into this.
Your first point along the traverse is Head Crater, which we call out 'f' (on the pre-planned traverse).

What we would like to do, in view of the fact that you are going out towards the ALSEP, is to move that site over to the northwest rim of Head Crater; and coordinates there are R-0, 11.0 (drawn at 12.0 on the Houston map).
And then, you will carry out what we already have outlined for Head Crater.
That's the two partial pans across Head Crater and document the slumps and ledges.

In addition to that, seeing as we have the PSE (Passive Seismic Experiment) so closely located to that, we would like to see if we can get a known signal for the PSE; so, if possible, could you roll a large crater - (correcting himself) a large boulder - the former would have been a little bit harder.
Roll a large rock into the crater and take a stereopair of the rock rolling...
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Take a stereopair of the rock prior to rolling and a stereopair of the track made by the rock after rolling.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Laughing) Hey, we've had a lot of training for that sort of thing on the geology trips we had.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Still laughing) We're with you all the way.
Let's press on from the Head.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Laughing; with a good deal of Houston laughter audible in the background) Roger.
We've got some happy looking geologists here.
We have Uel Clanton back here, and he's betting that somewhere along the traverse, you'll find some "stuff".
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Laughing) I think there's stuff all over the place.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: After this first point, then you can go on out to ALSEP.
And word on the CCIG is to make sure that it is laying on its back.
We would like for you to confirm, however, that you did try to lay it down in the normal mode and that the only way you thought you could make it work is having it lay on its back.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Look, we are going to go the other way around, I think.
Let's go to ALSEP and then to (Station) 1.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, let me tell you another thing while you're standing there.
I want to tell you, I do have Bench Crater in view from the window; Sharp Crater, I do not.
So it looks to me like it would be relatively easy to go to ALSEP (and then) to the coordinates you gave me on Head Crater.
And I am looking at it (Head Crater) right now, and I see several rocks which might do what you want us to do (and) which we might be able to roll down the side of that crater.
And followed by one astronaut, probably.
But anyhow, we will give that a whirl.
And then, is the next point you want us to go Sharp?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The next one is Bench Crater and then we'd be moving on to Sharp.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: What we would like to do is to move...
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: ...I'd kind of like to do it the other way around and that way I am going around in a circle.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: What we wanted to do was to move your Point "b" on Bench Crater on over to the northwest edge of that, as opposed to on the southwestern edge.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Give me the coordinates and we'll do it your way.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Coordinates on that would be M-0 and 10.0, so you would be up on the north side.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That is where I was poised (with his pencil).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, three things we would like you to do which are in addition to what we already discussed on your plan.
Take stereopairs of the features of interest in Bench Crater, especially of the bench; determine whether the bench is bedrock or breccia near the base of the regolith.
And, if the bench is bedrock, sample the ejecta representative of the bench or sample the bench itself, if possible.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And lastly, in Bench Crater, look northwest and southwest from the rim of Bench Crater to see if Copernican ray material is obviously different from other units.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Moving on to Sharp Crater, which is coordinate A.
First, we call for a full trench site sample in the crest of Sharp Crater; and we want to make sure you also add to that the gas analysis sample.
That looks as though it will be pretty much your furthest point out (from the LM).
We'd like a full pan from the rim of Sharp Crater; that, also, is because that is your furthest point out.
And, crew option at this point, extend your traverse west into what appears to be Copernicus ray material (if any is visible) and also...
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Hold on for a second, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Last point on Sharp Crater is sample and describe differences across the contact of M-1/M-2 (as is marked in the upper left of the map), if it is apparent when you reach that region.
(Pause) On your map, that shows up as a dotted line running northwest-southeast.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Pause) I can tell you right now, it is going to be pretty darn hard to do that.
(If) you're looking cross-Sun, the material looks all the same.
Looking down-Sun, it looks all the same, except it's a different color (than the cross-Sun color).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I'm sure that you can see the stuff from far out; but down here, it might as well all be the same until you get right up on top of an individual rock.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Probably you might not see any color differences, but if you could keep your eye open for differences in rock types (and/or textures).
(Pause) Moving on to the fourth point...
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: ...which is Halo Crater, as we have it called out there.
At this point, you can try to join the two core tubes together and core through the thin ejecta of Crater 6, or Halo Crater.
When you do that, you'll have to pull the pip pin off the one core tube which you make the bottom tube.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We'd like you to avoid the rockiest parts of the crater;...
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: ...and, if the tubes can't be joined, just take one on the rim and then one about 100 feet west of that location.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: If you could, give us a pan at that location.
And here is a comment which is really applicable to all of the traverse.
Document patterned ground and fillets on different slopes and blocks, especially any asymmetric fillets you may run into.
We would expect...Well, we would find it most interesting to get this type of information on the youngest material so that's why we call for it here, especially in Halo Crater.
The best way to document patterned ground is to photo into the Sun, near-field, and that way the pattern should show up in an optimum way.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And the last one (that is, the last activity on the proposed traverse) is you go on down the Surveyor Crater, and then in there we have Block Crater.
We'd like, there (at Block Crater), to collect the samples of major rock types and a partial pan across the Surveyor Crater.
And I think that covers it from our end.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We may have a little trouble getting to Block Crater.
I'm not sure whether it is an optical illusion or what; but that wall that the Surveyor is on looks one whale of a lot steeper than 14 degrees.
Now, it just may be that we are standing on the other side of the crater ourselves and it just looks a little funny.
And we've been discussing the Surveyor a little bit here during the evening.
And that crater gets pretty rugged over on that side, especially at the Block (Crater) area, as I remember it from yesterday.
But we'll give her a go.
Now, when we get in each one of these points, you can remind us of it again.
I think we have it fairly well in mind what you want.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, we'll be talking to you on the way, and one last note of clarification on that CCIG.
If it's sitting up so that it's pointing horizontal to the ground (which it is), just leave it alone.
If it's flipped over so that it's looking into the ground, then we want you to lay it on its back.
If it's already on its back, that's good enough.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Now, the cover should be off; is that right?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: If it's not off, should we try and take it off for you?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Long Pause while Gibson gets an answer from the Principal Investigator)
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: If it's on there, leave it as it is.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Do you want a core tube at Head Crater, or do you want us to skip that one?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, Al; let's look for that third core tube over at Sharp Crater.
Take that in doing your trench site sampling.
That will allow you to get that biological core tube sample at that point.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We're having a changeover of sites here and we may be off the line for a short while.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That's a good way to do it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: It'll be in about another 3 minutes that we're handing over.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: A real interesting thing has happened to that solar wind collector.

It's been sitting out there since yesterday, of course; and, when I left if yesterday, it was just a flat sheet of foil - you know - restrained at both ends; but as I look out there now, starting maybe a foot from the top, it has sort of folded back around the pole that's holding it.
Looks almost like a sail in the wind around the pole.
It's sort of bulging in the front and being bent back on the sides.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We've got a fairly strong solar wind, I suspect, Al.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: You may think you're kidding; I don't know.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I won't think you'd be kidding.
It could be, maybe, the front part of that is just thermally expanding a lot more than the back.
The back's probably radiating and the front's probably very hot.
And just a thermal difference across it could do it.
I'm meeting with a lot of approval back here on that idea.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Yeah, well, it looks like it's wrapped around the pole...That's the funny thing.
It looks like the wind is blowing on it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Well, we've got two good ideas, so far.
Maybe we can come up with a third.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: The (Primary ECS) LiOH canister was changed out at 130:00:00.
EVA Prep is almost complete to PLSS donning.
We're just putting the stuff (an anti-fog agent) on our visors.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Will you confirm that you did keep the bracket on the engine cover for TV camera stowage rather than putting it in the jettison bag - that is the ECS LiOH (garbled).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: You better believe it; it's still there.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Intrepid, one additional word on the CCIG.
When you're...When you go out to the CCIG, we will leave the power on to the instrument and we'd like you to tell us what its status is before you touch it.
If the status is such that we want it left in that configuration, you'll just move on.
If you have to change its configuration, then we'll have to turn the power off and we'd like you to hold off going near it until we give you the clearance.
We'll give you a reminder of this when you're on the way out.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Pete, Jane (garbled) sends her congratulations for a job well done.
And Al, Sue has followed it all and is thrilled that you've really made it on the money.
The children are fine; tired but happy, and they're going to continue following all the way through the second EVA.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We'll be coming up on the PLSS Comm in about 3 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I just went to FM and TV and was unable to contact you.
Had you all switched over to FM?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We've got the TV going right now, also.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (To Bean, who can be heard faintly responding to this comm checklist) VHF A Transmit/Receive; B, Receive; (now on Sur-69) LMP S-Band, T/R; ICS, T/R; Relay, On; Mode, VOX; (Pause) VHF A, Transmit/Receive; VHF B, Receive.
Here come your Comm: VHF, Voice, On, Off, On, Off, High; Range, Off/Reset; Squelch A and B noise threshold plus 1-1/2; (Tape) recorder, On.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: LMP connect to PLSS Comm, audio CB.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Read you loud and clear, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Should have a (warning) tone on, a vent flag, a P (flag), and a Press flag O.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: PLSS O2 Press gauge greater than 75 (percent).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We have a good Comm check with me.
I'm sorry, we don't need that.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (With) PLSS mode, Commander to B, you get no MSFN reception.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I read you loud and clear.
I got a press O flag.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I got an O2 flag and I've got 80 percent (oxygen).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Vent flag P and a Press flag O.
Usually have an O2 (flag) to begin with, do you?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I've got an O2, Press, and a vent.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: O2 will go out in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: PLSS mode...I'll go B and you go A.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: PLSS mode, both of them, AR.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Intrepid, we read you both loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay, Houston, LMP's O2 quantity is 80 percent.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We are opening the TV circuit breaker right now.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: CB (panel) 16 (garbled, probably "ECS") Cabin Repress, Close; Verify.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Suit Circuit Relief is in Auto.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Now, if you'll turn the other way, I'll get your other side.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: LM Crew: ...and H2O Sep light, and an ECS light.
transcript.speaker: Unknown

tokens: Down here just a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Why can't I get that to snap?
Can't see, that's...(Pause) Okay, that's all snapped.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Do you want to get...Oh, wait a minute; one other thing, right here.
I snapped your OPS hose in the side of the PLSS there.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Did you trim this thing out here?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Let me do that to you now.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And we throw in a couple of purge valves then.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Yes, I got the purge valves right now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And let me disconnect you from your (LM) suit hoses.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: You're going to have to do that; I can't see it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Get it started and rotate it with...And then counter-rotate.
(Pause) That's locked and that's locked; while I'm down here, let me...That's locked.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Safety's in; safety's in; safety's in and that safety's in.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Long Pause; audible click) You got it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Hold still and I'll put this (
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: A bit cool in here today.
Give you a little chilldown with this
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Garbled) first, let me get it all down in the back and everything.
Okay, hysters (that is, hoisters or self-doffing straps).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: In looking at the TV, it appears we'll be leaving the TV off for this, so if you would keep that circuit breaker out and go to S-Band mode PM.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We'll do it in just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Houston, I'm sure sorry the TV didn't work.
It's a beautiful sight to see Intrepid and Surveyor sitting here on this crater.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'll be waiting for those (70mm) pictures.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Here's a pair of gloves for you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: "PLSS fan on; don helmet and visors." Okay.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And you might as well get the TV and S-band reconfigured.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: There's PM and (garbled) (Pause) "Disconnect LM (H2O) hoses and
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And I've got my (LM H2O) hoses off.
How come we don't have our gloves on yet?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Can't see the checklist, that's all.
(Pause) Go ahead and get all snakied-up (that is, get the PLSS hoses
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, my PLSS water is hooked up.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay; and I'm ready to do the hoses, (that is, stow the LM water hoses in
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Get yours routed and hand them to me and I'll stick them in.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Pause) Wait a minute, I've got to get over there (garbled).
(Long Pause) (What) am I hung up against?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I don't know; let me see.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I can't move in for some reason.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Move over my way just a little way.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Never mind, I'm going to do it with these.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You're kind of hung up on the flight data file.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: This is going to do it better anyhow.
(Should have) done it that way yesterday.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Here's a pair of gloves for you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let me grab that checklist again.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: "Verify the following: helmet and visors, locked and adjusted."
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I can see that you are locked.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I've been over these a couple of times, every safety...
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: It's in; it's locked; pins are flush.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, don EV gloves and lock them up.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Okay, here's a pair for you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The right one of mine didn't want to do it either.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Can't tell whether they're in or not.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (To Pete) Pull this one open (for me).
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Doesn't want to do it, does it?
I can't do it with your...(Pause)

tokens: The one time you don't want something to hang up (garbled) it's (the bubble helmet) going to start fogging up.
There you go; let me look at it.

tokens: My pump's On and I'm beginning to be sucked down.

tokens: Put it back on the engine cover that way.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let me get my CB (garbled under Pete).
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I got my CBs real quick.
(Pause, jumping the next open breaker) One, 'out'; three, 'in'; one 'out'.
Those (the last three at the left end of row 1) are 'in' .

(Pause as he checks rightward from the left end along row 2) (Suit) fan 1 is 'out' (the breaker in the second row marked with a star in the diagrammeaning that he is to open the breaker), Cabin fan 1 is 'out'.
Glycol Auto Transfer is all in.
(Skipping the two 'in' PGNS breakers) And (the last breaker on the right end of row 2) 'out'.
(Moving up to row 3, working from the right end) Two, 'in'.
All the way out to Sig(nal) Conditioner (which is 'in').
(Garbled, but he is looking at the Rendezvous Radar Standby breaker which is 'in', and the last breaker at the left end of row 3) 'out'.
(Now on row 4) All 'out' but Mission Timer.
Hey, these guards are really good.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Ready for a Press Integ(rity) check.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Oh boy, does that feel better (with the suit partially inflated and no longer hanging on him)!
(Pause) Got a O2 "O" flag; Press O flag.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: ...the crew is ready for work; and Yankee Clipper's reporting for duty, sir.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Your two friends are moving right ahead.
They're just about ready to egress.
They're running about an hour and a half ahead of time.
They're pretty anxious to get out.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Mine decided to regulate at 3.9 (psi) for some reason.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I've got to get my O2 off (to start the pressure integrity check).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'll reset (the Digital Event Timer, or DET).
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: The decay checks look pretty good.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) You are Go for EVA, Pete.

tokens: Okay, I'm going to get my ox(ygen back on).

tokens: What are you going to do?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: (Pause) There, I got a tone, and an O(2 flag, as expected).

tokens: Wait a minute; it's not all the way.

tokens: I'm going to drop the cabin (pressure) now through the forward

tokens: Everything looks good down here, Pete.

tokens: Just let her bleed for a while.

tokens: All right, let her go for a while.

tokens: These suits are bleeding down, too.

tokens: Think I can get her now, Pete.

tokens: (Chuckling) All that stuff whistled out the door.
